content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 30 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 115r–116v | **Source Images:** DEU_030_page_001.jpg, DEU_030_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 162 | **Images:** DEU_030_vaticanus_page_001.jpg

---

**1** "And when all these things come upon you, the blessing and the curse, which I have set before you, and you call them to heart among all the nations where the LORD your God has driven you,

**2** and return to the LORD your God, you and your children, and obey his voice in all that I command you today, with all your heart and with all your soul,

**3** then the LORD your God will restore your fortunes and have compassion on you, and he will gather you again from all the peoples where the LORD your God has scattered you.

**4** If your outcasts are in the uttermost parts of heaven, from there the LORD your God will gather you, and from there he will take you.

**5** And the LORD your God will bring you into the land that your fathers possessed, and you shall possess it. And he will make you more prosperous and numerous than your fathers.

**6** And the LORD your God will circumcise your heart and the heart of your offspring, so that you will love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul, that you may live.

**7** And the LORD your God will put all these curses on your foes and enemies who persecuted you.

**8** And you shall again obey the voice of the LORD and keep all his commandments that I command you today.

**9** The LORD your God will make you abundantly prosperous in all the work of your hand, in the fruit of your womb and in the fruit of your livestock and in the fruit of your ground. For the LORD will again take delight in prospering you, as he took delight in your fathers,

**10** when you obey the voice of the LORD your God, to keep his commandments and his statutes that are written in this Book of the Instruction, when you turn to the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul.

**11** "For this commandment that I command you today is not too hard for you, neither is it far off.

**12** It is not in heaven, that you should say, 'Who will ascend to heaven for us and bring it to us, that we may hear it and do it?'

**13** Neither is it beyond the sea, that you should say, 'Who will cross to the beyond the sea for us and bring it to us, that we may hear it and do it?'

**14** But the word is very near you. It is in your mouth and in your heart, so that you can do it.

**15** "See, I have set before you today life and good, death and evil.

**16** If you obey the commandments of the LORD your God that I command you today, by loving the LORD your God, by walking in his ways, and by keeping his commandments and his statutes and his rules, then you shall live and multiply, and the LORD your God will bless you in the land that you are entering to take possession of it.

**17** But if your heart turns away, and you will not hear, but are drawn away to worship other gods and serve them,

**18** I declare to you today that you shall surely perish. You shall not live long in the land that you are going over the Jordan to enter and possess.

**19** I call heaven and earth to witness against you today, that I have set before you life and death, blessing and curse. Therefore choose life, that you and your offspring may live,

**20** loving the LORD your God, obeying his voice and holding fast to him, for he is your life and length of days, that you may dwell in the land that the LORD swore to your fathers, to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, to give them."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**LOGOS — Deuteronomy 30**

**"Restore your fortunes" (v. 3):** *ve-shav YHWH Elohekha et-shevutkha* — the phrase *shav shevut* (or *shuv shevut*) is a classic Hebrew idiom for restoration. Literally "he will return your captivity/turning," with *shevut* possibly connected to *shevut* ("captivity/exile") or *shuv* ("turning/return"). I render "restore your fortunes" as the standard idiom for this restoration formula, which encompasses both material and spiritual renewal. The LXX has *iasthai kyrios tas hamartias sou* (ἰάσεται κύριος τὰς ἁμαρτίας σου) in some versions — "the LORD will heal your sins" — though Vaticanus reads more closely to the Hebrew with *epistrepsai kyrios* language. The ambiguity in the LXX tradition is noteworthy.

**"Circumcise your heart" (v. 6):** *u-mal YHWH Elohekha et-levavekha ve-et levav zar'ekha* — "and the LORD your God will circumcise your heart and the heart of your offspring." The metaphor of circumcised heart appears also in Deuteronomy 10:16 as a command to Israel; here in 30:6 it becomes a divine promise — God himself will accomplish the inward transformation that the law demands. This theological trajectory is foundational for later prophetic hope (Jer 31:31–34; Ezek 36:26) and for Paul's argument in Romans 2:29. The LXX renders *peritemenei YHWH ho Theos sou ten kardian sou* (περιτεμεῖ κύριος ὁ θεός σου τὴν καρδίαν σου), exactly matching the Hebrew.

**"Not too hard for you / not far off" (v. 11):** *lo niflet hi mimekha ve-lo rechokah hi* — "it is not too wonderful/difficult for you and not far off." The word *niphleth* (נִפְלֵאת) is from *pala'*, which normally means "wonderful, marvelous, extraordinary" — but in context means something beyond human capacity or comprehension. I render "not too hard" as the contextually demanded meaning. The LXX has *ouk hyperogkes* (οὐχ ὑπέρογκος, "not excessive/too great"), capturing the sense of something beyond ordinary capacity.

**"It is in your mouth and in your heart" (v. 14):** *ki qarov elekha ha-davar me'od be-fykha u-vi-levavekha la'asoto* — "for the word is very near to you, in your mouth and in your heart to do it." The "word" (*ha-davar*) refers to the entire body of instruction, not a single commandment. The triad of accessibility — near, in the mouth, in the heart — counters any claim of inaccessibility (in heaven, beyond the sea). Paul quotes this verse in Romans 10:8 with christological application, reading "the word" as "the word of faith." The LXX has *en to stomati sou kai en te kardia sou kai en tais chersin sou* (ἐν τῷ στόματί σου καὶ ἐν τῇ καρδίᾳ σου καὶ ἐν ταῖς χερσίν σου) — notably the LXX adds "and in your hands," a plus not found in the Masoretic text of Leningrad Codex. This LXX addition ("in your hands") reflects either a different Hebrew Vorlage or an interpretive expansion emphasizing doing.

**"Life and death, blessing and curse" (vv. 15, 19):** The chapter presents the covenant as a binary choice: *ha-chayyim ve-ha-tov ve-ha-mavet ve-ha-ra'* (life and good, death and evil, v. 15) and *ha-chayyim ve-ha-mavet ha-berakhah ve-ha-qelalah* (life and death, blessing and curse, v. 19). The doubling — two pairs in two verses — emphasizes the absoluteness of the choice. The LXX preserves the paired structure with *ten zoen kai ton thanaton* (τὴν ζωὴν καὶ τὸν θάνατον), "life and death."

**"I call heaven and earth to witness" (v. 19):** *ha'idoti vakhem ha-yom et ha-shamayim ve-et ha-arets* — the invocation of heaven and earth as witnesses is a covenant-treaty formula common in Hittite suzerainty treaties, where cosmic powers or the gods serve as witnesses to treaty obligations. Here the cosmos itself testifies against Israel if they violate the covenant. The LXX has *martyromai hymin semeron ton te ouranon kai ten gen* (μαρτύρομαι ὑμῖν σήμερον τόν τε οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν), exactly equivalent.

**"He is your life and length of days" (v. 20):** *ki hu chayyekha ve-orekh yamekha* — a remarkable formulation: the LORD himself is identified not merely as the giver of life but as life itself and as the lengthening of days. This near-identification of God with life echoes the "I am" language of the Psalms and John 14:6. The LXX has *houtos gar he zoe sou kai he makrotes ton hemeron sou* (οὗτος γάρ ἡ ζωή σου καὶ ἡ μακρότης τῶν ἡμερῶν σου, "for this one is your life and the length of your days"), closely equivalent.

**"Therefore choose life" (v. 19):** *u-vacharta ba-chayyim* — "and you shall choose life." The Hebrew uses the perfect/prophetic perfect here, which can be read as an imperative or a prophetic certainty. The context of the appeal strongly suggests an imperative: "choose!" This is the hinge of the entire book of Deuteronomy — after forty chapters of instruction, the choice is set plainly before the people. The LXX renders *eklexai ten zoen* (ἔκλεξαι τὴν ζωήν), using the aorist imperative, confirming the volitional, commanding force.

— LOGOS
